nimbleModel
  • Home

implementation

Implementation of graph, index, and calculation relationships.

  • An indexRange manages the index content of one dimension of one variable.

    • This is implemented as a class hierarchy, with subclasses for scalar, sequence (e.g., 2:10), matrix (i.e., arbitrary).
    • Multiple index slots that are tied together are handled via indexRangeMatrixs.

  • A varRange manages a set of indexRanges representing all dimensions (‘slots’) of a variable.

    • A varRange also keeps track of how each indexRange is crossed or combined with others to represent the set of variable elements correctly.
    • Ultimately we may want to be able to collapse multiple varRanges to a single varRange when possible.

  • An indexRule manages the determination of LHS indices from RHS indices for one or more indexRanges. (Sometimes multiple index dimensions need to be solved jointly.)

    • Kinds of indexRules include ‘all’, ‘arbitrary’, ‘block’, and ‘constant’.
    • Inputs and outputs to application of an indexRule are indexRanges.
    • Inputs that are out of range do not produce output
    • Includes checks for going out of range
    • By reversing the order of a declaration, we can create both parent and child rules
    • Various possibilities for either input or output include no indexing, constant scalar, constant vector, arbitrary, and block.

  • An originalIndexingRule manages the determination of nodeFunction indices from actual index values, e.g., with a declaration such as y[i+1] <- x[i], determining that calculation for y[2] is handled with i=1.

    • Input is a varRange and output is also a varRange but where that represents a range of indices not of a variable.

    • This is handled by the indexRule functionality by making dummy declarations such as w[i,j] <- y[i+j] if the focal declaration is, say, y[i+j] <- x[i] + x[j].

  • A graphRule represents an edge in the model graph using a set of indexRules, for the purpose of graph queries. It manages the set of indexRules needed to determine all output indices from all input indices for the graph edge created by one of the input variables and the output variable of a single declaration.

    • Inputs and outputs to a graphRule are varRanges.
    • graphRule also can contain indexConstraints that don’t map to an output index but need to be enforced, such as y[i] <- x[2].
    • Dynamic indexing is handled similarly to as in current nimble – all possible dependencies are included in the rule.

  • A varRules object manages the set of rules (multiple graphRules or multiple of a given kind of nodeRules) for a single variable.

    • Collections of a given kind of rules for multiple variables done via a named list of varRules.

  • A nodeRule manages a single set of nodes from a declaration that are equivalent in some sense. Unlike a graphRule, a nodeRule does not translate indexing between variables; rather it simply represents the valid index values for a variable and distinguishes which index slots index over nodes (compared to within a node).

    • The nodeRuleClass is base class for declRuleClass, calcRuleClass, and rhsRuleClass
    • Has external and internal rules for handling i and 1:3 in y[i,1:3]. Rules are graphRules (though without the notion of two sides).
    • Input is a varRange.
    • Output is a nodeRange.
    • Indexing is over nodes (see comments below regarding nodeRange).

  • A nodeRange compactly represents a set of equivalent nodes.

    • E.g., y[i, 1:3] for i in 1:500 or y[i, j] for i in 1:5, j in 1:20
    • It is a subclass of varRange that knows which indexRanges are external (i in y[i,1:3]) and internal (1:3 in y[i,1:3].
    • Various API calls (e.g., getNodes, getDependencies) will work with either varRanges or nodeRanges transparently to the user.

  • A declRule manages indexing and calculation information from a single declaration.

    • Generates the nodeFunction, calcFun, as function of a vector of scalar-valued index arguments (e.g., calcFun(idx_vector))
    • Contains originalIndexingRule with indexing information needed for calculations to pass along to related calcRules.
    • Created by initial pass through model, with LHS from the declarations.
    • Notion of “equivalence” in terms of sort order, type, etc. is pushed down into calcRules.

  • A rhsRule manages information about RHS-only nodes.

    • Produced from ‘original’ rhsRules generated from all RHS terms in a declaration and subsequent modification (via exclude()) of those rules based on all or portions of the RHS appearing in a different LHS rule (a calcRule).

  • A calcRule manages a single set of nodes that can be calculated jointly.

    • Inherits from the nodeRule base class as a calcRule generally represents subsets of nodes containing elements of all the same type (e.g., top, end).
    • An individual calcRule is produced from the elements in a declaration that are tied in terms of their sort order and (are all of the same type).
    • When passed a varRange (or a nodeRange) it returns a calcRange (or nothing if there is no overlap) that can be used for calculation.
    • Includes parent/child links.
    • Produced from original calcRule generated by each declRule and subsequent fracturing of those rules (using makeCalcRules, which calls fracture()) based on all graph edges. (Noting that theta[i] ~ dnorm(theta0[i], 1) are not necessarily all latent or all top nodes, as it depends on how theta0[i] are declared; this involve splitting an ‘original’ calcRule when graph processing is done.)
      • everything on LHS starts out as both top and end
      • top found recursively determining which calcRules have stochastic parents using setStochParent.
      • end found by recursively determining which calcRules have stochastic dependents using setStochDep.
      • latent found by what is not top or end
    • sortID (topological sorting) determined by recursion based on walking the final set of calcRules (which have parent/child information) bottom-up (so that all data nodes are set to be tied, given data nodes generally more numerous than parameter nodes).
      • sortID can be a vector in state-space type cases.
    • Use of calcRules avoids having to store information in varStores and can use all of our tools for working with indexRanges and varRanges.

  • A calcRange manages the indexing for a single calculation for a variable.

    • Keeps track of declaration (via declRule, including associated nodeFunction, calcFun) and indexing (using originalIndexRanges).
    • Index values are based on original declaration context, e.g., a declaration of y[i+1] ~ dnorm(x[i], 1) with i=1:5 has calcRange indexing of 1:5 since nodeFunctions use i directly.
    • Need to discuss how to provide vectorized calculations.
      • Currently calcRange has a calculate method that loops over ranges of original indexes and calls declRule scalar-index-valued nodeFunction (calcFun).
      • Currently unclear how the compilation of this would work.
    • Includes sortID so that calcRange can be calculated in correct order relative to other calcRanges.

  • A dataRule manages information about data (or non-data) elements.

    • Application of such a rule produces a varRange.
    • Originally we had the idea of a varStore, but using a rule follows our general approach with the revamp and will allow easy filtering to data or non-data elements in getNodes or the like.

  • modelDeclClass objects represent information in a single declaration. This class has some of the same features as our previous BUGSdeclClass, but now contains a declRule and constituent upstreamRules and downstreamRules. While it is used when a modelDef is created, it is not generally used subsequently; instead sets of rules are aggregated (at the variable level rather than by declaration) as fields of the modelDef.

  • modelDefClass objects represent the model definition, in particlar the graphical structure. The core fields are the downstream and upstream graphRules and the various nodeRules.

  • [Needed? Not done] A copyRule manages the determination of copying for a variable

    • presumably trivial but a bit fuzzy at the moment
    • Input is a varRange?
    • Output is a varRange.
    • Perhaps all it needs to do is check for out of bounds and expand missing indexes?

  • (Deprecated?) A variableStore manages array-like information for variables (see below for alternative idea of using varTypeRules)

    • Stores information such as sort ID, isData, node types
    • Could be integer, logical, or character-valued
    • Should efficiently handle case where all values are the same
    • Ultimately may want to handle additional structure, such as blocks of equal values
      • Could have sets of blocks, with remaining values a “discontinuous block” if all the same.
    • Ultimately may want to handle simple sequential values (e.g., row-major or column-major in a matrix) as this could be useful for sort ID values in state-space/time-series contexts.
    • Should we have variableStores that map back to calcRules and nodeRules? This might avoid need to store something like node type if all node types for a given nodeRule are the same. Also avoids need for variable-level sort ID unless there is an ordering with a given calcRule (since could just look up sort ID of the calcRule).
